content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 19, 2010

Dixon Q. Dern, P.C.
1262 Devon Ave.
Los Angeles, California 90024

Re: Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. 1-10-109

Dear Mr. Dern:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your letter seeks general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act place any restrictions on the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences from offering state and local public officials complementary tickets to Emmy® award ceremonies and award ceremony dinners?

2. Is there any obligation under the Act on the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences as a ticket donor, to seek reimbursement from public officials for any portion of the value attributed to the tickets that exceed the gift limit?

CONCLUSION

1. All tickets offered for free to a public official by the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences are a gift under the Act and, if the official is required to report the Academy as a

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-10-109
Page No. 2


source of gifts on his or her Statement of Economic Interests, the tickets cannot exceed the Act's
current gift limit of $420 per calendar year.

2.     While not required to seek reimbursement, if the recipient reimburses the donor
for the full amount of the gift within 30 days after receipt, the gift is deemed to have not been
given, or if, within the 30-day period, a partial amount is reimbursed the gift value is reduced by
the amount of the reimbursement.

FACTS

You are the attorney for the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences ("Academy") and
are seeking advice on behalf of the Academy. The Academy is the organization that awards
Emmy® awards to persons and programs in the television industry. The Academy holds several
award ceremonies during which Emmy® awards are presented; the best known of these is the
Primetime Emmy Awards ceremony that is televised nationally.

For each ceremony the Academy makes tickets (both to the ceremony and to the dinner
that follows) available for purchase by designated potential purchasers (e.g. network
representatives, producers, etc.). The prices of tickets vary and are posted on the tickets. Seats
in the auditorium run from an estimated amount of $200 to $600 depending on whether the seats
are in the orchestra or less well positioned. The tickets for the dinners currently run around
$700. Additionally, the Academy gives complimentary tickets to nominees of the awards as well
as other members of the industry associated with the Academy. It has been the practice of the
Academy to offer complimentary tickets (usually 2 each) to elected local, state and in some cases
federal officials. These officials perform no official duties at the award ceremonies or award
dinners. The tickets are offered to public officials so that they may attend the event as audience
members. Usually, no price is indicated on these tickets, although, if sold, the prices would be
the same as for tickets that are sold. The academy does not employ lobbyists.

ANALYSIS

Gift Limits

The Act prohibits certain public officials, including elected state officers and elected
officers of local government agencies, from accepting gifts from any single source in a calendar
year with a total value of more than $420. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.)¹ A gift is
defined as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received." (Section 82028(a).) A single gift given
to both an official and one or more members of the official's family is a gift to the official for
the full value of the gift. (Regulation 18944.) Any person who makes a gift in violation of the gift

_______________
¹ Public officials holding positions listed in Section 87200 must report all sources of gifts, including the
Academy, on their Statement of Economic Interests. Otherwise, whether a public official must report a gift, and
whether the gift is subject to the $420 gift limit, depends on how that official is required to report gifts under his or
her agency's conflict-of-interest code (See Sections 87300-87313).

File No. I-10-109
Page No. 3


limit is also liable in a civil action for an amount of up to three times the amount of the unlawful
gift. (Section 89521.)

The Academy does not employ lobbyists, thus the Act does not restrict the Academy
from giving gifts to public officials that exceed the $10 calendar month gift limit on lobbyist
employers. (Section 86203.) However, if the Academy gives gift tickets to public officials in
amounts that exceed the $420 calendar year gift limit the Academy would be in violation of the
Act because Section 89521 imposes liability upon both the maker and the recipient of a gift that
is unlawful because it exceeds the gift limit.

Exceptions

The Act provides exceptions to the recipient of gifts and the gift limit. Based on your
facts, the following exceptions could apply. If within 30 days of receipt, a gift is returned to the
donor, donated unused to a charitable organization, or donated to a state, local, or federal
government agency, a gift is considered to have never been given or received under the Act. If
the recipient of a gift reimburses the donor for all or a portion of the gift, the value of the gift is
reduced by the amount of the reimbursement. (Regulation 18943.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl
